title: EU Politicians Investigated Pegasus Spyware. Then It Ended Up on One of Their Phones
gist: Citizen Lab found NSO Group's Pegasus spyware repeatedly infected the iPhone of Greek MEP Stelios Kouloglou while he served on the European Parliament's committee investigating Pegasus, exposing the EU's failure to adopt its own spyware safeguards years after recommending them.

insights.claim: Citizen Lab forensic analysis found that Greek MEP Stelios Kouloglou's iPhone was infected with NSO Group's Pegasus spyware multiple times while he served on the European Parliament's PEGA Committee investigating that very spyware.
insights.speaker: Lily Hay Newman
insights.so_what: The first confirmed PEGA member compromise shows spyware operators will target the investigators themselves, escalating the reputational and legal risk overhang for NSO and its new US owners.
insights.quote: it is the first time that a member of the PEGA Committee has been identified as having been a victim of the Pegasus spyware while they were working within the group.
insights.locator: The revelation that Kouloglou’s device was targeted

insights.claim: Kouloglou's phone was first infected on October 21, 2022 while he was hospitalized for elective surgery, then again on March 6-7, 2023 as the committee finalized its findings and negotiations.
insights.speaker: Lily Hay Newman
insights.so_what: Infections timed to key hearings and report negotiations suggest deliberate targeting of the committee's work product, not random surveillance.
insights.quote: Looking at the dates, it’s pretty obvious that somebody was not just randomly spying on him, but really targeted the committee’s work

insights.claim: Citizen Lab found no indication of Greek government involvement but identified overlaps between the attacks on Kouloglou and Pegasus use against seven Russian- and Belarusian-speaking journalists and activists between August 2020 and January 2023.
insights.speaker: Lily Hay Newman
insights.so_what: Infrastructure overlap hints at a shared operator without attribution, keeping the geopolitical accountability question, and NSO's export exposure, unresolved.
insights.locator: The research stops short of naming any government

insights.claim: Pegasus, first discovered by Citizen Lab in 2016, exploits an evolving set of mobile OS vulnerabilities to infect both iOS and Android, tapping microphones and cameras and grabbing messages, contacts, browsing data and photos.
insights.speaker: Lily Hay Newman
insights.so_what: Cross-platform zero-click capability means no consumer device is inherently safe, sustaining demand for mobile forensics and hardened-device vendors.
insights.locator: First discovered by Citizen Lab in 2016

insights.claim: Apple sent Kouloglou three spyware-targeting notifications in March 2023, August 2023 and April 2024, but the alerts are not issued in real time and he does not recall seeing them.
insights.speaker: Lily Hay Newman
insights.so_what: Delayed, easily-missed platform alerts expose a gap between detection and effective victim defense that dedicated forensic labs are meant to fill.
insights.locator: Citizen Lab found, as part of its forensic analysis

insights.claim: NSO Group, which developed Pegasus, was founded and remains headquartered in Israel, but US-based investors acquired a majority stake in the company in 2025.
insights.speaker: Lily Hay Newman
insights.so_what: US majority ownership of the world's most notorious spyware firm creates a direct policy and reputational conflict for a government that has sanctioned the sector.
insights.quote: United States–based investors acquired a majority stake in the company in 2025.
insights.locator: NSO, the developer of Pegasus, did not return

insights.claim: The EU investigation was triggered largely by the 2021 Pegasus Project leak, which showed at least 180 journalists were reportedly targeted, and coincided with Greece's 'Watergate' scandal involving Intellexa's Predator spyware against journalists and officials.
insights.speaker: Lily Hay Newman
insights.so_what: Multiple vendors (NSO, Intellexa) targeting European elites shows a competitive mercenary-spyware market, not a single bad actor, widening the regulatory target set.
insights.locator: The European investigation into the use of Pegasus

themes.theme: EU policy failure and AI escalation

insights.claim: The PEGA Committee's recommendations—including an EU-based forensic device analysis lab and an election spyware taskforce—remain unadopted years after the report, leaving European lawmakers unprotected.
insights.speaker: Lily Hay Newman
insights.so_what: Regulatory paralysis despite clear recommendations means the spyware threat in Europe is a policy-will problem, not a knowledge gap, and unlikely to resolve near term.
insights.quote: There is no lack of recommendations on how to fix it. It's just a matter of, can you please now do it?
insights.locator: There is no lack of awareness

insights.claim: Citizen Lab's Scott-Railton argues Europe's parliaments are unprepared for endemic spyware and warns AI will 'turbocharge the mercenary spyware threat by lowering costs and barriers to entry.'
insights.speaker: Lily Hay Newman
insights.so_what: AI-driven cost collapse expands the spyware operator base, a structural growth vector for the offensive-cyber market and a rising tail risk for governance and privacy.
insights.quote: It leaves Europeans unprotected even as AI promises to turbocharge the mercenary spyware threat by lowering costs and barriers to entry.
insights.locator: Europe has a mountain of spyware abuses

insights.claim: Scott-Railton notes that some countries, including the United States, have made progress against spyware through sanctions, visa bans, and executive orders.
insights.speaker: Lily Hay Newman
insights.so_what: US deterrence tools contrast with EU inaction, suggesting jurisdiction-specific regulatory divergence that vendors can arbitrage.
insights.locator: He notes, too, that some countries
